methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series D → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.5x) to $300M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 4 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Robotics & Manufacturing. Range: $1.0B - $2.3B.

private.name: Exotec
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series D), post-money $2B

data.narrative:
Path Robotics, a Series D robotics and manufacturing automation company founded by Andy and Alex Lonsberry, is evaluated against a peer universe of 9 private companies and 1 public comparable, spanning autonomous systems and industrial robotics infrastructure.

Two triangulation methods are applied. Stage-progression analysis uses the company's $300M+ cumulative raise as a baseline, applying a 1.5x multiplier typical of Series D-to-subsequent-stage advancement, yielding $450M. Peer-median valuation draws from four same-sector, same/adjacent-stage comparables: Exotec (Series D, $2.0B), Divergent (Series E, $2.3B), Hadrian (Series C, $1.6B), and Nimble Robotics (Series C, $1.0B). The median of this set is $1.8B, with a range of $1.0B to $2.3B. These two methods produce a wide spread reflecting uncertainty about Path's relative progress within its cohort.

Synthesizing both approaches yields a triangulated range of $382M to $2.1B, with a median of $1.1B. The substantial gap between stage-progression ($450M) and peer-median ($1.8B) reflects ambiguity around Path's revenue traction, unit economics, and market penetration relative to named peers. Key uncertainties include gross margins in manufacturing automation, customer concentration risk, and whether recent fundraising reflects valuation accretion or dilution dynamics. A credible range supporting a Series D round would likely cluster in the $600M–$1.4B band, pending disclosure of revenue run-rate and customer retention metrics.
